title: Bubble and squeak
subtitle: Leftover potato and greens fried into a crisp cake.
excerpt: Cold mashed potato and yesterday's cooked vegetables, sprouts or cabbage or peas, pressed into a cake and fried hard in dripping until the underside is crisp. The Boxing Day breakfast of the British Sunday-roast week.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), records the dish under "bubble and squeak", though the original referred to beef and cabbage rather than potato. The vegetable-and-mash version became standard in the post-war frugality of the 1940s.

recipe.yieldDescription: One large cake, cut into 4 wedges
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day after a roast, using the previous day's cold mash and cooked vegetables straight from the fridge.

content.text: Bubble and squeak takes its name from the sound the vegetables make as they fry. The dish is by definition leftovers cooking: cold mash, cold cooked sprouts, cold cooked cabbage, whatever's in the fridge from Sunday lunch. Pressed into a heavy frying pan with a knob of dripping or butter and left alone over medium-high heat until the underside catches a deep brown crust. Flipped, fried the other side, served with a fried egg and a teaspoon of brown sauce.
Two things matter. The mash and vegetables both need to be cold; warm mash falls apart in the pan. And the cake needs to be left alone long enough to develop a crust before you try to turn it.

items.prepNote: cooked, cold; or sprouts, kale, or whatever greens you have
or salted butter, or duck fat

content.text: A 26 cm heavy-based frying pan. Cast iron is ideal; the heavy base holds the heat and gives a properly crisp underside.
Melt half the {{beef-dripping}} of dripping in the frying pan over medium heat. Add {{onion}} of chopped onion and cook 8 minutes until soft and starting to colour. Tip into a large mixing bowl.

Roughly chop {{cabbage-savoy}} of cooked greens (cabbage, sprouts, kale, anything green). Tip into the bowl with the onion. Add {{potato}} of cold mash, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Mix with a wooden spoon until evenly combined.

Return the pan to the hob. Add the remaining dripping over medium-high heat. When it sizzles, tip the mixture in. Press down hard with the back of a fish slice into an even cake. Leave alone for 5 to 6 minutes. The underside should be deep brown when you lift an edge to check.

Slide the cake onto a plate, browned side up. Invert a second plate over the top and turn the whole thing over. Slide the cake back into the pan, brown-side up. Press down again. Cook for another 5 to 6 minutes until the second side is also deep brown.
Tip onto a board and cut into wedges. Scatter with parsley if using. Serve with fried eggs, leftover cold meat, and a wedge of grilled tomato.

items.cause: Mixture too wet, or flipped too early.
items.fix: Use cold day-old mash. Don't add liquid. Leave alone for the full 5 to 6 minutes so a proper crust forms before flipping.

items.cause: Heat too low, or wrong fat.
items.fix: Medium-high heat throughout. Beef dripping or duck fat gives the best colour; butter alone burns before it browns. Add a teaspoon of vegetable oil to butter if that's all you have.
50 g of dripping total is plenty for a 500 g cake. The fat is for browning, not soaking.

items.cause: Salt and pepper not generous enough.
items.fix: Leftover mash and cabbage both want serious seasoning when reheated. Taste the raw mixture before frying; it should taste properly salted before it goes in the pan.

content.text: Bubble and squeak is the British Boxing Day breakfast, the morning-after of every Christmas Day. The same logic produces colcannon in Ireland (with kale and spring onion) and rumbledethumps in Scotland (with cheese on top). Eat with fried eggs, cold ham, and a dollop of HP sauce, or a spoonful of piccalilli.
